---
title: "network_cleaning"
format: html
editor: visual
---

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
```

```{r}
# 1. Load the matrix you exported from Python
# matrix_data <- read_csv("kendrick_similarity_matrix.csv")
matrix_data <- read_csv("kendrick_openai_matrix.csv")
full_albums <- read_csv("kendrick_master_dataset.csv")
```

```{r}
# When Pandas exports a matrix, the first column containing the names 
# often gets a weird default name like "...1" or "Unnamed: 0". 
# Let's rename it to 'Source' for Gephi.
#colnames(matrix_data)[1] <- "Source"

# 2. Convert the square matrix into a Gephi-friendly Edge List
edge_list <- matrix_data %>%
  # This pivots the wide matrix into three columns: Source, Target, and Weight
  pivot_longer(cols = -Source, names_to = "Target", values_to = "Weight") %>%
  
  # Remove self-loops (a song's similarity to itself, which is always 1.0)
  filter(Source != Target) %>%
  
  # Remove duplicate pairs (Song A -> Song B is the same as Song B -> Song A)
  # This alphabetical sorting trick ensures we only keep one unique edge per pair
  filter(Source < Target) %>% 
  
  # THE HAIRBALL FILTER: Only keep edges where songs are highly similar
  # You will likely need to tweak this number! 0.70 is a great starting point.
  filter(Weight >= 0.7) 

# 3. Create the Node List 
# Gephi requires an 'Id' column. 'Label' is what shows up on the screen.
node_list <- tibble(
  Id = unique(c(edge_list$Source, edge_list$Target)),
  Label = Id
) %>% 
  left_join(full_albums, by = c("Id" = "track_name")) %>% 
  select(-lyrics)
```

```{r}
write_csv(edge_list, "gephi_edges.csv")
write_csv(node_list, "gephi_nodes.csv")
```
